# How the Internet Actually Works ### The Magic Behind the Screen Have you ever wondered... When you open YouTube, how does a video from another country appear on your screen in just a few seconds? When you send a WhatsApp message, how does it instantly reach your friend—even if they're thousands of kilometers away? When you search something on Google, how does Google know exactly what you asked? It feels almost magical. But behind every website, every message, every video, every online game, and every social media post is a giant system called **the Internet**. The interesting part? The Internet isn't one giant computer. It isn't a magical cloud floating somewhere in the sky. It's **millions of computers talking to each other every single second.** Whenever you open a website, stream a movie, send a message, or play an online game, your device starts a conversation with another computer somewhere else in the world. By the end of this article, you'll understand exactly how those conversations happen—and you'll never look at the Internet the same way again. ---
## 📚 What You'll Learn By the end of this guide you'll understand: - 🌍 What the Internet actually is - 🖥️ The difference between clients and servers - 📡 What an Internet Service Provider (ISP) does - 🛣️ How routers decide where data should travel - 🔢 Why DNS exists - 📦 How data is split into packets - ⚡ Why websites load so quickly - 🌊 Why undersea cables are more important than satellites No networking knowledge is required. We'll build everything step by step using simple analogies.
--- ## Imagine the Internet as a Global Postal System Before learning technical terms like DNS, IP addresses, routers, and packets... Let's imagine something much simpler. Suppose you want to send a birthday gift to your friend who lives in another city. Would you carry the package all the way yourself? Probably not. Instead... - You write your friend's address. - The local post office receives your package. - It passes through several sorting centers. - It travels across cities and highways. - Eventually it reaches your friend's home. The Internet works in almost the same way. Instead of delivering birthday gifts... It delivers **data**. Every photo you upload... Every email you send... Every Netflix movie you stream... Every Instagram Reel you watch... Every Google search you perform... Everything becomes **small pieces of digital information** traveling through many computers before finally reaching its destination. The only difference is speed. While a postal package may take days to arrive, Internet data usually completes its journey in **less than a second**. --- ## What Exactly Is the Internet? Here's the simplest definition you'll ever hear: **The Internet is simply a massive network of computers connected together.** That's it. No magic. No mysterious cloud. Just millions (actually billions) of devices connected together through cables, wireless networks, routers, switches, and data centers.
CORE CONCEPT: There is no magical cloud. Every website, photo, email, and video you access is stored on a real computer somewhere in the world. When people say "it's in the cloud," they actually mean "it's stored on someone else's computer."
Think about all the devices connected to the Internet. - Your laptop - Your phone - Smart TVs - Gaming consoles - Google's servers - Netflix servers - Amazon's servers - GitHub servers - Your college website - Millions of company computers Every one of them becomes part of one enormous global network. You can think of the Internet as **the world's biggest neighborhood**, where every computer has an address and can communicate with every other computer. Another interesting fact? The Internet isn't owned by one company. Google doesn't own it. Microsoft doesn't own it. Neither does any single government. Instead, thousands of Internet Service Providers, universities, technology companies, data centers, and organizations all connect their own networks together. Those interconnected networks are what we simply call **the Internet**. --- ## Meet the Main Characters Now that we know what the Internet is... Let's meet the important components that make everything work. Think of these as the main characters in our story. Every time you visit a website, all of them work together behind the scenes. --- ### 🖥️ Your Device (Client) Your computer, laptop, tablet, or smartphone is called a **client**. Why? Because it's the one requesting information. Its job is incredibly simple. It asks questions. For example: - "I want to open youtube.com." - "Show me today's weather." - "Download this image." - "Play this Spotify song." A client usually doesn't store the information you're looking for. Instead... It asks another computer to send it. In networking, we call this the **Client–Server Model**. The client asks. The server answers. Almost everything you do on the Internet follows this simple pattern. --- ### 🏢 The Server A server is also a computer. There's nothing magical about it. The difference is what it does. Instead of serving one person... It serves hundreds... Thousands... Or even millions of people at the same time. Think of a restaurant. Customers walk in and place orders. The kitchen prepares the food and sends it back. Servers work exactly the same way. Clients request information. Servers prepare the information. Then send it back. Different servers specialize in different tasks. For example: - 🎥 YouTube servers store videos. - 🎵 Spotify servers store music. - 📦 Amazon servers store product information. - 📧 Gmail servers store your emails. - 💻 GitHub servers store source code. - 🌐 Your college server stores the college website. Whenever you use an online service, you're almost always communicating with one or more servers somewhere in the world. These servers are usually located inside massive buildings called **data centers**, filled with thousands of computers running 24 hours a day. --- ### 🌍 ISP (Internet Service Provider) Now here's an important question. How does your laptop even reach Google's servers? It can't simply shout across the world. First... It needs someone to connect it to the Internet. That someone is called an **Internet Service Provider**, or **ISP**. Examples include: - Airtel - Jio - BSNL - AT&T - Verizon Think of your ISP as the highway connecting your home to the rest of the world. Without an ISP... Your computer would be isolated. It would be like living on an island with no roads connecting you to the outside world. Your ISP acts as the bridge between your home and the global Internet. --- ### 🛣️ Routers Imagine every city had roads... But no traffic signals... No road signs... No GPS... No intersections. Traffic would become complete chaos. That's exactly the problem routers solve. Routers are the Internet's traffic managers. Their job is to decide: **"Which path should this data take?"** Every second, routers make millions of decisions. They constantly look for the fastest, safest, and most efficient path to deliver data. If one route becomes busy or stops working, routers can choose another route almost instantly. Without routers, the Internet simply couldn't function. ---
## 🚀 Quick Recap Before following a real website request, remember these four important characters: 🖥️ **Client** → Requests information 🏢 **Server** → Provides information 🌍 **ISP** → Connects you to the Internet 🛣️ **Router** → Decides where data should travel Now let's watch all of them work together when you visit a website.

# What is Linux? ### The Hidden Foundation What if I told you that every time you use Google, watch Netflix, scroll Instagram, buy something on Amazon, or launch a rocket into space... there's a good chance Linux is working behind the scenes? But what exactly is Linux? ### The City Analogy Think of a computer as a city. * **Applications** are the people. * **Your files** are the buildings. * **The hardware** is the land. * **Linux** is the government that keeps everything running smoothly. It decides who gets access to the CPU, memory, storage, and devices, making sure every program gets what it needs without everything descending into chaos. ### Why do we need it? If Windows and macOS already exist, why did someone create Linux? In the early 1990s, most operating systems were either expensive, closed-source, or limited. A Finnish computer science student named Linus Torvalds wanted something different—an operating system that anyone could study, modify, and improve. That small personal project grew into Linux, which today powers everything from smartphones to supercomputers. ### Is Linux just another operating system? This is where many beginners get confused.
CORE CONCEPT: Linux is the kernel (the engine). An Operating System (like Ubuntu or Fedora) is the complete car. Windows is a complete package made by one company, while Linux is the shared core that others build upon.
### Where is Linux actually used? Do you think Linux is only for programmers? * **Android phones?** Linux kernel. * **Most web servers?** Linux. * **Cloud platforms?** Mostly Linux. * **Supercomputers?** Almost all Linux. * **Raspberry Pi?** Linux. * **Smart TVs and routers?** Often Linux. You may not use Linux directly every day, but you almost certainly rely on devices and services powered by Linux. ### Why do developers love Linux? Why do so many developers switch to Linux? * Full control over the system. * Powerful command-line tools. * Excellent environment for programming. * Highly customizable. * Free and open source. * Widely used on servers and in the cloud. ### Is Linux difficult? Do you need to be a hacker to use Linux? No. Linux isn't difficult because it's Linux. It feels difficult because it's different. The first time you switched from a bicycle to a motorcycle, it probably felt confusing too. Once you understand the basics, many people find Linux faster and more enjoyable than other operating systems. ### Should you learn Linux? Is learning Linux worth your time in 2026? If you're interested in software development, cybersecurity, cloud computing, DevOps, AI infrastructure, or system administration, Linux is one of the most valuable skills you can learn. It isn't just another operating system—it's a foundation that powers much of the modern internet.
"Stay curious. Most interesting things happen outside the syllabus."

# What Really Happens When You Open a Website In the previous article, we met the four main characters that power the Internet. - 🖥️ **Client** - 🏢 **Server** - 🌍 **Internet Service Provider (ISP)** - 🛣️ **Routers** Each one plays an important role whenever you browse the web. But we stopped just before the interesting part. We never actually watched them work together. So let's do exactly that. Imagine you're sitting in front of your laptop. You open your browser... Type: ```text google.com ``` And press **Enter**. The page appears almost instantly. To us... It feels effortless. But behind those few milliseconds lies an incredible chain of events involving computers, routers, data centers, and thousands of kilometers of fiber-optic cables. Let's follow that journey together. ---
## 🚀 Mission Throughout this article, we'll follow **one single request** from the moment you press **Enter** until Google's homepage appears on your screen. By the end, you'll understand exactly what happens behind every website you visit.
--- ## Step 1 — Your Browser Has a Small Problem When you type: ```text google.com ``` Your browser immediately encounters its first obstacle. It knows the **name**. But it has absolutely no idea where Google actually lives. Humans prefer names because they're easy to remember. Imagine trying to remember this every time you wanted to search something: ```text 142.250.183.78 ``` Not very practical. That's why we use names like: - google.com - youtube.com - github.com - openai.com They're designed for humans. Unfortunately... Networks don't understand names. They understand **IP addresses**. Every computer connected to the Internet has one. Before your browser can send even a single request... It first needs to discover Google's IP address. So it asks someone for directions. --- ## Meet DNS — The Internet's Phonebook Think about your phone for a moment. When you want to call your friend... You don't remember their phone number. You simply search for: **Priya** Your phone quietly finds the number for you. DNS works almost exactly the same way. Instead of storing people's names and phone numbers... It stores **domain names** and their corresponding **IP addresses**. Whenever you type: ```text google.com ``` Your computer sends a request to a **DNS Server**. The question is surprisingly simple. > **"Do you know the IP address for google.com?"** The DNS server quickly searches its records... Finds the correct IP address... And sends it back. Only now does your browser know where Google's servers actually are. Notice something interesting. You never had to know Google's IP address. Your browser handled everything automatically. This entire lookup usually takes only a few milliseconds. ---
CORE CONCEPT Humans navigate the Internet using **domain names**. Computers navigate the Internet using **IP addresses**. **DNS (Domain Name System)** acts as the translator between the two. Without DNS, you'd have to memorize numerical IP addresses for every website you wanted to visit.
--- ## A Tiny Shortcut You Never Notice Here's something even more interesting. Your browser doesn't always contact a DNS server. If you've recently visited the same website... Your computer may already remember its IP address. This temporary memory is called a **DNS Cache**. Instead of asking the DNS server again... Your browser simply reuses the stored address. Think of it like calling your best friend. The first few times, you search for their number. Eventually... You remember it by heart. Your computer does exactly the same thing. Caching helps websites load even faster by avoiding unnecessary DNS lookups. --- ## Step 2 — The Journey Finally Begins Now your browser knows Google's address. The destination has been found. It's finally time to send the request. But here's another question. How does your laptop actually reach a computer that might be thousands of kilometers away? It can't magically send data directly to Google's servers. Instead... Your request begins a long journey across the Internet. It leaves your browser... Travels to your home Wi-Fi router... Gets forwarded to your **Internet Service Provider (ISP)**... Then starts hopping across multiple routers around the world. Each router performs one simple job. It looks at the destination IP address... Finds the best available path... And forwards your request to the next router. Eventually... After passing through many different networks... Your request reaches Google's data center. What's fascinating is that your request doesn't travel along one giant cable directly to Google. Instead... It moves through many independent networks. Each router only worries about one thing: > **"Where should I send this next?"** No single router knows the entire journey. Each one simply forwards the request to the next best destination until it finally reaches Google's servers. ---
## 💡 Did You Know? When you load a modern website, your browser usually doesn't make **just one request**. It requests: - 📄 The HTML page - 🎨 CSS stylesheets - ⚙️ JavaScript files - 🖼️ Images - 🔤 Fonts - 🎥 Videos - 📦 API responses Each of these can follow a similar journey across the Internet. That's why browsers constantly send and receive dozens—or even hundreds—of requests in the background.
--- --- ## Step 3 — Why Websites Don't Travel as One Giant File At this point... Your request has finally reached Google's servers. The server understands exactly what you're asking for. Now it has to send the webpage back. But here's something that surprises many people. The server doesn't simply bundle everything into one enormous file and send it across the Internet. Instead... It does something much smarter. It **breaks the webpage into thousands of tiny pieces** before sending anything. These pieces are called **packets**. --- ## Why Split Everything Into Packets? Imagine you're moving to a new house. You own a large bookshelf. Would you carry the entire bookshelf on your back? Probably not. You'd take it apart... Pack the pieces into smaller boxes... Move each box separately... Then reassemble everything once you arrive. That's exactly how the Internet works. Instead of sending one giant block of data... It sends lots of smaller packets. Each packet contains only a tiny portion of the original information. Whether it's: - 📄 An HTML document - 🎨 A CSS file - ⚙️ JavaScript - 🖼️ Images - 🎥 Videos Everything eventually becomes packets. ---
CORE CONCEPT The Internet never thinks in terms of **webpages** or **videos**. It only knows how to move **packets**. Everything you use online—whether it's Netflix, Instagram, GitHub, or YouTube—is ultimately transmitted as thousands (or even millions) of packets.
--- ## What's Inside a Packet? Think of each packet as a tiny parcel being shipped through the postal system. Every parcel needs more than just its contents. It also needs a shipping label. A network packet is no different. Every packet contains two important parts. ### 📦 Payload This is the actual data. It could be: - Part of an image - Some HTML - A few lines of CSS - A JavaScript file - Even a tiny fragment of a YouTube video ### 🏷️ Header The header acts like the address label on a package. It contains information such as: - 📍 Source IP Address - 🎯 Destination IP Address - 🔢 Packet Number - ⚙️ Protocol Information - ✅ Error Checking Data Without this information... Routers wouldn't know where to send the packet. And your computer wouldn't know how to put everything back together again. --- ## Every Packet Travels Its Own Journey Here's where things become really fascinating. You might imagine every packet following exactly the same path. That's not always true. One packet might travel through Chennai. Another might travel through Mumbai. Another could even pass through Singapore before reaching its destination. The Internet doesn't force every packet to stay together. Instead... Routers make independent decisions for each one. Their only goal is simple. > **Find the fastest available path.** That means two neighbouring packets can take completely different routes across the world... ...and still arrive safely. ---
## 💡 Did You Know? The Internet doesn't reserve a dedicated road for your data. Instead, packets from **millions of people** travel together across the same cables. At any given moment... The same fiber-optic cable may be carrying: - Someone streaming Netflix - A student attending an online class - A gamer playing Valorant - A company making a bank transaction - You reading this very article All of these packets travel side by side without getting mixed up because every packet carries its own destination address.
--- ## Step 4 — Putting Everything Back Together Eventually... The packets arrive at your computer. But they don't always arrive in the same order they were sent. For example... The server might send them like this: ```text Packet 1 Packet 2 Packet 3 Packet 4 Packet 5 ``` But your computer could receive them like this: ```text Packet 3 Packet 1 Packet 5 Packet 2 Packet 4 ``` At first glance... That sounds like chaos. Fortunately... Every packet carries its own sequence number. Your browser patiently waits until enough packets have arrived. Then it rearranges them into the correct order. Only after that does it begin rendering the webpage. This entire process usually happens so quickly that you never notice it. To you... The page simply appears. --- ## The Server's Reply Completes the Journey Once Google's server has finished preparing the webpage... It sends thousands of packets back across the Internet. Those packets travel through multiple routers... Cross several different networks... Eventually reach your ISP... Pass through your home router... And finally arrive at your browser. Within a fraction of a second... Thousands of packets have travelled across cities... Countries... And sometimes even continents... Just so your browser can display one webpage. But this raises another interesting question. How can data travel between continents so quickly? Does every request really bounce off satellites in space? Not quite. The real answer lies deep beneath the ocean. --- ## Step 5 — The Internet's Invisible Highways By now... Your browser has successfully received thousands of packets from Google's servers. But there's one question we haven't answered yet. **How did those packets travel across the world so quickly?** Many people imagine that Internet traffic is constantly bouncing off satellites floating in space. It sounds reasonable. After all... Satellites help with GPS, television broadcasts, and weather forecasting. Surely the Internet works the same way... Right? Surprisingly... **Not for most of the Internet.** In reality, more than **95% of international Internet traffic** travels through **undersea fiber-optic cables** stretched across the ocean floor. These cables are the true backbone of the modern Internet. --- ## Beneath the Ocean Lies the Internet Imagine connecting every continent with thousands of kilometers of incredibly thin glass cables. Now imagine shining pulses of light through those cables at nearly the speed of light. That's exactly how fiber optics work. Instead of sending electrical signals like traditional copper wires... Fiber-optic cables transmit data using **light**. Every packet you've learned about travels as tiny flashes of light through these fibers. These cables stretch across oceans, connecting countries and continents together. Whether you're: - 🌏 Browsing a website hosted in the United States - 🎥 Watching a YouTube video from Japan - 💻 Pushing code to GitHub - 🤖 Using ChatGPT There's a very good chance your data is travelling through one or more of these underwater cables. ---
CORE CONCEPT When people say "the Internet is everywhere," they often imagine satellites. In reality, the Internet is mostly a **massive network of fiber-optic cables** running under streets, across countries, and beneath oceans. These cables carry enormous amounts of data every single second.
--- ## Why Not Use Satellites? If satellites already exist... Why don't we simply send all Internet traffic through them? The answer comes down to one word: **Latency.** A signal sent to a satellite has to travel thousands of kilometers into space... Then back down to Earth. That extra distance introduces noticeable delays. Fiber-optic cables, on the other hand... Provide: - ⚡ Lower latency - 🚀 Faster transmission speeds - 📦 Higher bandwidth - 💰 Lower operating costs That's why companies like Google, Meta, Microsoft, Amazon, and many telecom providers invest billions of dollars building and maintaining undersea cable networks. Satellites are still useful... Especially in remote locations... But for the majority of global Internet traffic... Fiber optics are the preferred choice. --- ## What Happens If a Cable Breaks? At this point, you might be wondering... "If so much of the Internet depends on these cables... What happens if one of them gets damaged?" It's a good question. And surprisingly... Cable failures happen more often than you might think. Ships dragging anchors... Fishing equipment... Earthquakes... Even underwater landslides... Can damage these cables. Would that disconnect the Internet? Thankfully... No. --- ## The Internet Was Built to Handle Failure One of the Internet's greatest strengths is that it was never designed around a single path. Instead... It behaves more like a giant road network. Imagine you're driving to another city. Halfway through your journey... You discover that the highway has been closed. Do you give up and go home? Of course not. You simply follow a different road. The Internet works exactly the same way. Every router continuously exchanges information with neighboring routers. If one connection suddenly becomes unavailable... Routers quickly calculate another available path. Your packets are automatically redirected. Most of the time... You won't even notice it happening. ---
## 💡 Did You Know? Every second... Millions of routing decisions are made across the Internet. Routers are constantly checking: - Which path is the fastest? - Which path is congested? - Which path is unavailable? - Which route should packets take next? This dynamic decision-making allows the Internet to continue operating even when parts of the network fail.
--- ## Putting Everything Together Let's revisit everything we've learned. Imagine opening your browser one more time. You type: ```text google.com ``` Here's what happens behind the scenes. 1. 🌐 Your browser asks a DNS server for Google's IP address. 2. 📍 DNS returns the correct IP address. 3. 📤 Your browser creates an HTTP request. 4. 🏠 The request leaves your device and reaches your home router. 5. 🌍 Your ISP forwards the request into the global Internet. 6. 🛣️ Multiple routers forward the request toward Google's data center. 7. 🌊 If necessary, the request crosses oceans using undersea fiber-optic cables. 8. 🏢 Google's servers receive the request. 9. 📦 The webpage is split into thousands of packets. 10. 🚀 Those packets travel back across the Internet. 11. 🧩 Your browser reassembles every packet into the original webpage. 12. 🎉 Google's homepage appears on your screen. All of this usually happens in well under **one second**. ---
## 📚 What We Learned Throughout this two-part journey, we've explored how the Internet works from the ground up. ✅ The Internet is a massive network of interconnected computers. ✅ Clients request information while servers provide it. ✅ ISPs connect our devices to the global network. ✅ Routers decide the best path for every packet. ✅ DNS translates domain names into IP addresses. ✅ Data travels as packets rather than one large file. ✅ Fiber-optic cables connect countries and continents. ✅ The Internet automatically reroutes traffic when failures occur. The next time you open a website... Remember that behind a single click lies one of the most complex engineering systems humanity has ever built.
--- # Final Thoughts The Internet often feels like magic. Websites appear instantly. Messages arrive within seconds. Videos stream without interruption. But now you know the truth. Behind every click... There's a remarkable collaboration between clients, servers, DNS, routers, fiber-optic cables, and millions of packets travelling across the globe. The next time someone asks, > **"How does the Internet actually work?"** You'll know it's not magic. It's one of the greatest engineering achievements ever created. And now... You understand the journey of how data travels.
